title: Baltimore · Down From the Peak
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2020 + BPD NIBRS 2022–2026 · 278 neighborhoods · real locations

hook.line: Baltimore crime, from the 1995 peak to 2020
hook.sub: FBI UCR · then the map, month by month, up close

punchline.sub: −71% under the same FBI measure · the city's own count begins in 2022

copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2020 the gray bars are the FBI's classic 'index' crimes; from 2022 the figures are BPD's own NIBRS Group A records, deduplicated from victim rows to incidents — a broader count, so the level steps up. Two years are blank on purpose: 1999 (a broken FBI reporting year — 503 reported vs ~70,000 either side) and 2021 (BPD's NIBRS transition left no full-year total). Gaps stay gaps, nothing estimated. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly incidents, 2022–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location, as published by BPD, sampled.
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 278 neighborhoods — every dot a real reported location, straight from the source.
copy.methodRecentSub: since 2022 · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident location as published by BPD. 99.9% map to one of 278 neighborhoods — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: BPD publishes one row per victim; every count shown is incidents, deduplicated by complaint number (259,819 victim rows → 224,602 incidents). FBI UCR (through 2020) and BPD NIBRS (2022+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Baltimore neighborhood reports the fewest Group A crimes?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Baltimore Police Department (NIBRS Group A) via Open Baltimore · real reported locations
copy.coverageText: 99.9% of incidents mapped to a neighborhood
copy.creditsSources: Sources: Baltimore Police Department NIBRS Group A victim-based data (2022–present) via Open Baltimore, deduplicated to incidents by CCNumber, & FBI UCR (1985–2020) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (ORI MDBPD0000). Neighborhoods: City of Baltimore official 2010 statistical areas. Two years are omitted and disclosed, never interpolated: 1999 (broken FBI reporting year) and 2021 (BPD NIBRS-transition year, no full-year total). FBI UCR index crimes and BPD NIBRS incidents are different measures, shown as two chapters and never equated. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 1985: 66,781 index crimes — where the FBI record begins.
2000: 66,397 — the fall is underway. (1999 is blank — a broken FBI reporting year, not a real drop.)
2020: 28,048 — down 71% from the 1995 peak; the last FBI year.
2023: 57,833 — the high point of BPD's own victim-deduped count.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported incident location, as published by BPD open data.
July 2023 — the busiest Group A month since 2022: 5,743.
Downtown — Baltimore's busiest neighborhood for reported Group A crime since 2022.
Mount Holly: down 44% since 2022 — the city's biggest neighborhood drop.
Reported Group A is roughly flat citywide: 45,735 (2022) → 47,233 (2025), +3.3%.
